annotations.label: Tensor

anchor.snippet: a tensor is an algebraic object that describes a multilinear relationship

annotations.note: Mathlib's `TensorProduct` over modules formalizes the algebraic tensor object describing multilinear relationships, generalizing the vector-space case.

annotations.label: Tensor field

anchor.snippet: A family of tensors, that vary across space in this way, is a tensor field

annotations.note: No `TensorField` declaration exists in Mathlib; tensor fields as sections of bundles over a manifold are not formalized.

annotations.label: Tensor as multidimensional array

anchor.snippet: A tensor may be represented as a (potentially multidimensional) array

annotations.note: Mathlib has no notion of a tensor defined as an indexed multidimensional array of components.

annotations.label: Order, degree, rank of a tensor

anchor.snippet: The total number of indices is also called the order , degree or rank of a tensor

annotations.note: The order/degree/rank index count of a tensor is not formalized as a defined invariant in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Contravariant transformation law

anchor.snippet: This is called a contravariant transformation law

annotations.note: Component transformation laws under change of basis are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Covariant transformation law

anchor.snippet: This is called a covariant transformation law

annotations.note: Component transformation laws under change of basis are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Linear operator is a (1,1)-tensor

anchor.snippet: the tensor corresponding to the matrix of a linear operator has one covariant and one contravariant index

annotations.note: `dualTensorHomEquiv : Dual R M ⊗ N ≃ₗ (M →ₗ N)` identifies linear operators with (1,1)-tensors for finite free modules.

annotations.label: Transformation law for an order p+q tensor

anchor.snippet: The transformation law for an order p + q tensor with p contravariant indices and q covariant indices

annotations.note: The explicit (p,q)-component transformation law is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Tensor of type (p,q) (array definition)

anchor.snippet: A tensor of type ( p , q ) is an assignment of a multidimensional array

annotations.note: Mathlib does not define type-(p,q) tensors via basis-indexed arrays plus transformation rules.

annotations.label: Tensor as equivariant map (GL(n) representation)

anchor.snippet: An equivalent definition of a tensor uses the representations of the general linear group

annotations.note: The definition of a tensor as a GL(n)-equivariant map on frames is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Space of frames is a principal homogeneous space for GL(n)

anchor.snippet: Then F is a principal homogeneous space for GL( n )

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Basis` and `AddTorsor` but does not establish the set of frames as a GL(n)-torsor.

annotations.label: Tensor as multilinear map

anchor.snippet: a type ( p , q ) tensor T is defined as a multilinear map

annotations.note: `MultilinearMap` formalizes multilinear maps; a type-(p,q) tensor is the special case of a multilinear map on copies of V and its dual.

annotations.label: Double dual isomorphism in finite dimensions

anchor.snippet: This linear mapping is an isomorphism in finite dimensions

annotations.note: `Module.evalEquiv : M ≃ₗ Dual R (Dual R M)` gives the canonical double-dual isomorphism for reflexive modules, of which finite-dimensional spaces are a case.

annotations.label: Tensor as element of a tensor product

anchor.snippet: A type ( p , q ) tensor is defined in this context as an element of the tensor product of vector spaces

annotations.note: `TensorProduct` defines elements of a tensor product of modules, generalizing the (p,q)-tensor-as-element definition over vector spaces.

annotations.label: Correspondence between tensor-product and multilinear-map definitions

anchor.snippet: the universal property of the tensor product gives a one-to-one correspondence between tensors defined in this way and tensors defined as multilinear maps

annotations.note: `PiTensorProduct.lift` is a linear equivalence between multilinear maps and linear maps out of the tensor product, encoding exactly this universal-property correspondence.

annotations.label: Natural isomorphism of tensor spaces in finite dimensions

anchor.snippet: the spaces of tensors obtained by each of these constructions are naturally isomorphic

annotations.note: Mathlib provides natural equivalences like `dualTensorHomEquiv` for finite free modules but not a general theorem that all tensor constructions yield naturally isomorphic spaces.

annotations.label: Tensor field (differential geometry)

anchor.snippet: such an object is called a tensor field , often referred to simply as a tensor

annotations.note: Tensor fields on manifolds are not formalized as a dedicated object in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Dot product as a tensor

anchor.snippet: An elementary example of a mapping describable as a tensor is the dot product

annotations.note: `Matrix.dotProductBilin` packages the dot product as a bilinear map, i.e. a (0,2)-tensor.

annotations.label: Cauchy stress tensor

anchor.snippet: A more complex example is the Cauchy stress tensor T

annotations.note: The Cauchy stress tensor of continuum mechanics is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cross product is not a tensor

anchor.snippet: The cross product , where two vectors are mapped to a third one, is strictly speaking not a tensor

annotations.note: `crossProduct` is formalized as a bilinear map, but the claim that it is a pseudovector rather than a genuine tensor is not formalized.

annotations.label: Bilinear form and inner product as (0,2)-tensors

anchor.snippet: a bilinear form is the same thing as a (0, 2) -tensor

annotations.note: `LinearMap.BilinForm` is defined as `M →ₗ M →ₗ R`, which is precisely a (0,2)-tensor.

annotations.label: Raising an index yields an (n+1,m-1)-tensor

anchor.snippet: Raising an index on an ( n , m ) -tensor produces an ( n + 1, m − 1) -tensor

annotations.note: The index-raising operation and its effect on tensor type are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Contraction yields an (n-1,m-1)-tensor

anchor.snippet: Contraction of an upper with a lower index of an ( n , m ) -tensor produces an ( n − 1, m − 1) -tensor

annotations.note: `contractLeft`/`contractRight` formalize the dual-module pairing (a (1,1)→scalar contraction) but not the general (n,m)→(n-1,m-1) index bookkeeping.

annotations.label: Invariants preserved under change of basis

anchor.snippet: there are invariants of tensors that must be preserved under any change of the basis

annotations.note: Basis-independent invariants such as `LinearMap.trace` exist, but a general theory of tensor invariants under change of basis is not formalized.

annotations.label: Type (valence) of a tensor

anchor.snippet: determine the type (or valence ) of the tensor, a pair of natural numbers

annotations.note: Mathlib does not attach a numeric type/valence pair (p,q) to a tensor.

annotations.label: Order of a tensor

anchor.snippet: The order (also degree or rank ) of a tensor is thus the sum of the orders of its arguments

annotations.note: The order of a tensor as the sum p+q is not formalized as an invariant in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Scalars, vectors, matrices as tensors of order 0,1,2

anchor.snippet: Scalars are simple numbers and are thus 0th-order tensors

annotations.note: The identification of scalars/vectors/matrices as order 0/1/2 tensors is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Tensor algebra

anchor.snippet: The collection of tensors on a vector space and its dual forms a tensor algebra

annotations.note: `TensorAlgebra R M` formalizes the tensor algebra of a module, generalizing the mixed tensor algebra on V and its dual.

annotations.label: Ricci calculus

anchor.snippet: Ricci calculus is the modern formalism and notation for tensor indices

annotations.note: Ricci calculus is an index notation, not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Einstein summation convention

anchor.snippet: The Einstein summation convention dispenses with writing summation signs

annotations.note: Mathlib uses explicit `Finset.sum`; the Einstein summation convention is not implemented.

annotations.label: Penrose graphical notation

anchor.snippet: Penrose graphical notation is a diagrammatic notation which replaces the symbols for tensors with shapes

annotations.note: Penrose graphical/diagrammatic notation is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Abstract index notation

anchor.snippet: The abstract index notation is a way to write tensors such that the indices are no longer thought of as numerical

annotations.note: Abstract index notation is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Component-free notation

anchor.snippet: A component-free treatment of tensors uses notation that emphasises that tensors do not rely on any basis

annotations.note: Mathlib's treatment is inherently basis-free, but "component-free notation" as a named concept is not a formalized statement.

annotations.label: Addition and scalar multiplication of tensors

anchor.snippet: two tensors of the same type may be added together, and that tensors may be multiplied by a scalar

annotations.note: The `AddCommMonoid` and `Module` instances on `M ⊗[R] N` give addition and scalar multiplication of tensors of the same type.

annotations.label: Tensor product operation

anchor.snippet: The tensor product takes two tensors, S and T , and produces a new tensor

annotations.note: `TensorProduct.tmul` (`⊗ₜ`) is the operation producing a new tensor from two given tensors.

anchors.snippet: The tensor product takes two tensors, S and T , and produces a new tensor

anchors.value: {\displaystyle (S\otimes T)_{j_{1}\ldots j_{k}j_{k+1}\ldots j_{k+m}}^{i_{1}\ldots i_{l}i_{l+1}\ldots i_{l+n}}=S_{j_{1}\ldots j_{k}}^{i_{1}\ldots i_{l}}T_{j_{k+1}\ldots j_{k+m}}^{i_{l+1}\ldots i_{l+n}}.}

annotations.label: Type of a tensor product

anchor.snippet: If S is of type ( l , k ) and T is of type ( n , m ) , then the tensor product S ⊗ T has type

annotations.note: The additivity of (p,q)-types under tensor product is not formalized since tensor type is not tracked in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: If S is of type ( l , k ) and T is of type ( n , m ) , then the tensor product S ⊗ T has type

anchors.value: {\displaystyle (S\otimes T)_{j_{1}\ldots j_{k}j_{k+1}\ldots j_{k+m}}^{i_{1}\ldots i_{l}i_{l+1}\ldots i_{l+n}}=S_{j_{1}\ldots j_{k}}^{i_{1}\ldots i_{l}}T_{j_{k+1}\ldots j_{k+m}}^{i_{l+1}\ldots i_{l+n}}.}

annotations.label: Tensor contraction

anchor.snippet: Tensor contraction is an operation that reduces a type ( n , m ) tensor to a type ( n − 1, m − 1) tensor

annotations.note: `contractLeft`/`contractRight` formalize contraction via the natural dual-module pairing, the core case of tensor contraction.

annotations.label: Contraction using the metric tensor

anchor.snippet: the term contraction is used for removing two contravariant or two covariant indices by forming a trace with the metric tensor

annotations.note: Metric contraction of two like indices is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lowering an index

anchor.snippet: This operation is quite graphically known as lowering an index

annotations.note: `BilinForm.toDual` maps a vector to a covector via a nondegenerate form, formalizing the metric flat (index-lowering) isomorphism on a single index.

annotations.label: Raising an index

anchor.snippet: the inverse operation can be defined, and is called raising an index

annotations.note: The inverse `(BilinForm.toDual hB).symm` maps a covector back to a vector, formalizing index-raising on a single index, but not the general tensor case.

annotations.label: Stress, strain and elasticity tensor fields

anchor.snippet: The stress tensor and strain tensor are both second-order tensor fields

annotations.note: Stress, strain and elasticity tensor fields of continuum mechanics are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Stress as a type (2,0) tensor

anchor.snippet: This is described by a tensor of type (2, 0) , in linear elasticity

annotations.note: The stress as a type-(2,0) tensor in linear elasticity is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Tensors in physics

anchor.snippet: Common applications include

annotations.note: Physical applications of tensors (electromagnetic, permittivity, etc.) are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Trifocal tensor generalizing the fundamental matrix

anchor.snippet: with the trifocal tensor generalizing the fundamental matrix

annotations.note: The trifocal tensor and fundamental matrix of computer vision are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Nonlinear medium and susceptibility tensors

anchor.snippet: the medium is termed nonlinear

annotations.note: Optical susceptibility tensors of nonlinear media are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Tensor in machine learning (multidimensional array)

anchor.snippet: a tensor is the same thing as a multidimensional array

annotations.note: Mathlib does not define the machine-learning notion of a tensor as a multidimensional array.

annotations.label: Rectangular matrix as a tensor

anchor.snippet: an example of a tensor in this context is a rectangular matrix

annotations.note: `Matrix m n α` formalizes rectangular matrices, but they are not framed as tensors in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Order-d tensor over different vector spaces

anchor.snippet: an order- d tensor may likewise be defined as an element of a tensor product of d different vector spaces

annotations.note: `PiTensorProduct` (`⨂[R] i, s i`) is exactly an element of a tensor product of an indexed family of distinct modules.

annotations.label: Generalization of tensors to infinite dimensions

anchor.snippet: The notion of a tensor can be generalized in a variety of ways to infinite dimensions

annotations.note: Mathlib's algebraic `TensorProduct` already applies to infinite-dimensional modules, but the various analytic generalizations are not collected as a single statement.

annotations.label: Scalar density

anchor.snippet: Such a quantity that scales by the reciprocal of the absolute value of the determinant of the coordinate transition map is called a scalar density

annotations.note: Scalar densities and their transformation by the determinant are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Tensor density and weight

anchor.snippet: A tensor density transforms like a tensor under a coordinate change

annotations.note: Tensor densities and their weights are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Current density as a tensor density

anchor.snippet: An example of a tensor density is the current density of electromagnetism

annotations.note: The electromagnetic current density as a tensor density is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Tensor as a geometric object

anchor.snippet: This makes a tensor a special case of a geometrical object

annotations.note: The notion of a geometric object generalizing tensors is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Spinor

anchor.snippet: A spinor is an object that transforms like a tensor under rotations in the frame

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes the `spinGroup` inside a Clifford algebra but has no spinor object or spinor space.

annotations.label: Spinors as elements of the spin representation

anchor.snippet: Spinors are elements of the spin representation of the rotation group

annotations.note: `spinGroup` is formalized as a submonoid of the Clifford algebra, but its spin representation and the spinors it acts on are not.
